827	\subsection{The affine base-change lemma and its remaining obligations}
828	
829	The affine base-change lemma below proceeds \emph{directly on global sections}: it
830	reduces, via the locality criterion
831	Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens} and the two affine dictionaries
832	Lemmas~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso} and~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso},
833	to two obligations that are isolated here as named blocks. Both are extracted from
834	the proof of Stacks ``Affine base change'', and both are absent from the pinned
835	Mathlib. The first is the \emph{locality reduction}: restricting to the affine opens
836	of \(S'\) reduces the isomorphism claim to the affine--affine model
837	\(S = \operatorname{Spec}(R)\), \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(R')\),
838	\(X = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\). The second is the
839	\emph{section-level identification}: in that affine--affine model, the global-sections
840	incarnation \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) of the base-change map, transported through the two
841	proved tilde dictionaries, is the canonical cancellation isomorphism
842	\(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\) — an isomorphism with no flatness hypothesis. This
843	is the honest cut of the affine close: locality on \(S'\), then a pure computation on
844	the module of global sections. No separate abstract adjoint-mate identification is
845	performed at the level of sheaves; the mate is unwound \emph{on sections}, where it
846	becomes \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\) directly.
847	
848	\begin{lemma}
849	
850	\leanok
851	[Affine-local compatibility of the base-change map]
852	  \label{lem:base_change_map_affine_local}
853	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_map_affine_local}
854	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}
855	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
856	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
857	  % "local on $S$ and $S'$" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
858	  % L920--926).
859	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "The statement is local on $S$ and $S'$. Hence we may
860	  % assume $X = \Spec(A)$, $S = \Spec(R)$,
861	  % $S' = \Spec(R')$ and $\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}$
862	  % for some $A$-module $M$."
863	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
864	  change'' (proof, local-on-\(S\)-and-\(S'\) step).}
865	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be an affine morphism and \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
866	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, with a base-change square as above. The formation of
867	  the base-change map \texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap}
868	  (Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map}) is compatible with restriction
869	  to affine opens of \(S'\): for each affine open \(U \subseteq S'\), the
870	  restriction of the base-change map to sections over \(U\) agrees with the
871	  base-change map of the square obtained by restricting along \(U\). Consequently,
872	  by the affine-open locality criterion
873	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}, the assertion that the base-change
874	  map is an isomorphism over an arbitrary base-change square \((S, S', X, X')\)
875	  reduces to the affine--affine case \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(R)\),
876	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(R')\), \(X = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\),
877	  \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) for an \(A\)-module \(M\).
878	
879	  \medskip
880	  \emph{Intended formalized signature.} With the ambient data
881	  \(f : X \to S\), \(g : S' \to S\), \(f' : X' \to S'\), \(g' : X' \to X\) and a
882	  proof \(h\) that the square is cartesian (\(h : \mathrm{IsPullback}\ g'\ f'\ f\ g\),
883	  whose commutativity \(h.w : g' \circ f = f' \circ g\) feeds the base-change map),
884	  under the hypotheses \([\mathrm{IsAffineHom}\ f]\) and
885	  \([\mathcal{F}.\mathrm{IsQuasicoherent}]\), the lemma is the locality reduction
886	  \[
887	    \bigl(\forall\, U : S'.\mathrm{affineOpens},\
888	      \mathrm{IsIso}\ \bigl((\texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap}\ f\ g\ f'\ g'\ h.w\
889	        \mathcal{F}).\mathrm{app}\ U\bigr)\bigr)
890	    \;\Longrightarrow\;
891	    \mathrm{IsIso}\ \bigl(\texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap}\ f\ g\ f'\ g'\ h.w\
892	      \mathcal{F}\bigr),
893	  \]
894	  i.e.\ it discharges the affine-open locality criterion
895	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens} for the base-change map, with the
896	  per-affine-open hypothesis being exactly the affine--affine section assertion that
897	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange} supplies. The content
898	  beyond the bare locality criterion is the identification of the restriction of
899	  \texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap} to an affine open \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(R')
900	  \subseteq S'\) with the base-change map of the affine--affine square cut out over a
901	  chosen affine \(\operatorname{Spec}(R) \subseteq S\) containing \(g(U)\), over which
902	  \([\mathrm{IsAffineHom}\ f]\) makes \(X\) restrict to \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and
903	  \([\mathcal{F}.\mathrm{IsQuasicoherent}]\) makes \(\mathcal{F}\) restrict to
904	  \(\widetilde{M}\).
905	\end{lemma}
906	
907	\begin{proof}
908	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}
909	  \leanok
910	  By the affine-open locality criterion
911	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}, the morphism
912	  \texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap} is an isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules
913	  as soon as its restriction to the sections over each affine open \(U \subseteq S'\)
914	  is an isomorphism. The only content beyond the bare criterion is to identify that
915	  restriction with the base-change map of the affine--affine square cut out over
916	  \(U\). We obtain the identification by unfolding the definition of the map and
917	  pushing the restriction functor through each of its building blocks. The
918	  compatibility this needs is \emph{not} a new coherence theorem: it is the naturality
919	  of the adjunction transpose, together with the standard fact that pushforward
920	  commutes with restriction to an open of the base. We spell this out in three steps.
921	
922	  \medskip
923	  \emph{Step 1: unfold the map.} By
924	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map}, \texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap}
925	  is the image, under the \((g^*, g_*)\)-adjunction transpose — the natural bijection
926	  \(\operatorname{Hom}(g^*A, B) \cong \operatorname{Hom}(A, g_*B)\) — of the morphism
927	  \[
928	    f_*\mathcal{F} \xrightarrow{\,f_*(\eta_{\mathcal{F}})\,}
929	    f_*(g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F} = g_*\,f'_*(g')^*\mathcal{F},
930	  \]
931	  where \(\eta_{\mathcal{F}} : \mathcal{F} \to (g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the unit of
932	  the \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction and the identification
933	  \(f_*(g')_* = g_*\,f'_*\) is the pseudofunctoriality of pushforward applied to the
934	  square's commutativity \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\). Thus the map is, by
935	  construction, the transpose of the \(f_*\)-image of a unit — a composite of
936	  adjunction data and pushforward functors, with no ingredient beyond these.
937	
938	  \medskip
939	  \emph{Step 2: restriction to \(U\) commutes with each building block.} Fix an affine
940	  open \(U \subseteq S'\) and write \(\rho_U\) for restriction to \(U\) —
941	  equivalently, the section functor \(\Gamma(U, -)\) on \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules,
942	  or pullback along the open immersion \(U \hookrightarrow S'\). Restriction along
943	  \(U\) is a functor, and every ingredient of Step 1 is a natural transformation or a
944	  natural bijection; so applying \(\rho_U\) to the transpose-of-a-unit of Step 1
945	  returns the transpose-of-the-unit built from the restricted data. Concretely:
946	  \begin{itemize}
947	    \item[(a)] the unit \(\eta_{\mathcal{F}}\) of the \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction
948	      restricts to the unit of the corresponding adjunction on the restricted square,
949	      because units are natural in their object and pulling the cartesian square back
950	      along \(U \hookrightarrow S'\) yields again a cartesian square (with total space
951	      the open \((f')^{-1}(U)\) of \(X'\));
952	    \item[(b)] \(f_*\) of a restricted morphism is the restriction of \(f_*\) of the
953	      morphism, because pushforward commutes with restriction to an open of the base:
954	      for an open \(U\) of the base one has
955	      \((f_*\mathcal{G})|_U = (f|_{f^{-1}U})_*\bigl(\mathcal{G}|_{f^{-1}U}\bigr)\),
956	      naturally in \(\mathcal{G}\) — and likewise for \((g')_*\) and \(f'_*\);
957	    \item[(c)] the \((g^*, g_*)\)-adjunction transpose is a bijection natural in both
958	      variables, so it commutes with restricting its source and target along \(U\).
959	  \end{itemize}
960	  Chaining (a), (b), (c) — i.e.\ transporting the transpose-of-unit composite of
961	  Step 1 through the restriction functor \(\rho_U\) and replacing each block by its
962	  restricted counterpart — shows that
963	  \((\texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap}\dots).\mathrm{app}\,U\), the restriction of the
964	  base-change map to sections over \(U\), is itself \texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap},
965	  now formed from the cartesian square restricted over \(U\). Choosing an affine open
966	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R) \subseteq S\) containing the image \(g(U)\), affineness of
967	  \(f\) (local on the base and stable under base change) makes the total space
968	  restrict to an affine \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), and quasi-coherence of
969	  \(\mathcal{F}\) makes it restrict to a tilde-module \(\widetilde{M}\) there; so this
970	  restricted map is exactly the base-change map of the affine--affine square cut out
971	  over \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(R')\) and \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\). The whole
972	  identification is thus definitional plus the naturalities (a)--(c); it introduces no
973	  obligation beyond them.
974	
975	  \medskip
976	  \emph{Step 3: conclude the reduction.} By Step 2 the per-affine-open hypothesis
977	  required by the locality criterion Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens} —
978	  that \((\texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap}\dots).\mathrm{app}\,U\) is an isomorphism
979	  for every affine open \(U \subseteq S'\) — is, under the identification of Step 2,
980	  precisely the affine--affine section assertion. That assertion is supplied, with
981	  \emph{no} flatness hypothesis, by the section-level computation
982	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange}, which identifies the
983	  affine--affine base-change map on global sections with the cancellation isomorphism
984	  \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\). Applying the locality criterion
985	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens} to this family of per-open
986	  isomorphisms gives \(\mathrm{IsIso}(\texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap}\dots)\), which
987	  is the asserted locality reduction.
988	\end{proof}

1140	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_mate_codomain_read}
1141	  \uses{lem:pullback_fst_snd_specMap_tensor, lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso,
1142	        lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
1143	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
1144	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
1145	  % "Namely X' = Spec(R' \otimes_R A) and F' is the qcoh sheaf associated to
1146	  % (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
1147	  % L929--932).
1148	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Namely, $X' = \Spec(R' \otimes_R A)$ and
1149	  % $\mathcal{F}'$ is the quasi-coherent sheaf associated
1150	  % to $(R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M$."
1151	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
1152	  change'' (proof, ``\(X' = \operatorname{Spec}(R' \otimes_R A)\)'' step).}
1153	  In the affine--affine model, with \(f' = \operatorname{pullback.snd}\) and
1154	  \(g' = \operatorname{pullback.fst}\) the legs of the pullback square, the global
1155	  sections of the codomain \(f'_*(g')^*\widetilde M\) are canonically
1156	  \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\) as an \(R'\)-module. Explicitly, the assembled
1157	  isomorphism
1158	  \[
1159	    \Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}} :
1160	    \Gamma\bigl(f'_*(g')^*\widetilde M\bigr)
1161	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M
1162	  \]
1163	  is the composite of the pullback dictionary Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}
1164	  (for \((g')^*\)) followed by the pushforward dictionary
1165	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso} (for \(f'_*\)).
1166	\end{lemma}
1167	
1168	\begin{proof}
1169	  \uses{lem:pullback_fst_snd_specMap_tensor, lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso,
1170	        lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
1171	  Here the two functors \((g')^*\) and \(f'_*\) are taken along the pullback cone legs,
1172	  which are \emph{not} a priori \(\operatorname{Spec}\)-maps; this is where
1173	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_fst_snd_specMap_tensor} enters. By that lemma
1174	  \(g' = \operatorname{pullback.fst}\) is, up to \texttt{pullbackSpecIso}, the
1175	  \(\operatorname{Spec}\)-map of the inclusion \(A \to R' \otimes_R A\), and
1176	  \(f' = \operatorname{pullback.snd}\) is the \(\operatorname{Spec}\)-map of
1177	  \(R' \to R' \otimes_R A\). With the legs so identified the affine dictionaries apply:
1178	  the pullback dictionary Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso} along
1179	  \(A \to R' \otimes_R A\) reads \((g')^*\widetilde M\) as the extension of scalars
1180	  \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\), and the pushforward dictionary
1181	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso} along \(R' \to R' \otimes_R A\) reads
1182	  \(f'_*\) of it as restriction of scalars back to \(R'\). The result is
1183	  \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\) as an \(R'\)-module, and composing the two dictionary
1184	  isomorphisms is \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\). This matches the Stacks description
1185	  \(X' = \operatorname{Spec}(R' \otimes_R A)\), with \(\mathcal{F}'\) the tilde of
1186	  \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\).
1187	\end{proof}

1191	[Generator trace of the section-level base-change map]
1192	  \label{lem:base_change_mate_generator_trace}
1193	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_mate_generator_trace}
1194	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:base_change_mate_domain_read,
1195	        lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read}
1196	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
1197	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
1198	  % "boils down to the equality" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
1199	  % L933--938).
1200	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the
1201	  % equality
1202	  % $$
1203	  % (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M
1204	  % $$
1205	  % as $R'$-modules."
1206	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
1207	  change'' (proof, ``boils down to the equality'' step).}
1208	  With the domain and codomain pinned by Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_domain_read}
1209	  and Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read}, the conjugated section-level
1210	  base-change map
1211	  \[
1212	    \Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}} \circ \Gamma(\alpha) \circ \Theta_{\mathrm{src}}^{-1} :
1213	    R' \otimes_R M \longrightarrow (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M
1214	  \]
1215	  sends \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (r' \otimes 1) \otimes m\).
1216	\end{lemma}
1217	
1218	\begin{proof}
1219	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:base_change_mate_domain_read,
1220	        lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read}
1221	  By Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map}, \(\alpha\) is the
1222	  \((g^* \dashv g_*)\)-transpose of \(f_*(\eta')\), with \(\eta'\) the
1223	  \(((g')^*,(g')_*)\)-unit. We trace a generator through the three pieces, reading each
1224	  functor on global modules via the dictionaries of
1225	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_domain_read} and
1226	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read}.
1227	  \begin{itemize}
1228	    \item[(1)] \emph{The \(((g')^*,(g')_*)\)-unit \(\eta'\).} Under the dictionaries
1229	      \((g')^* = (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A (-)\) (extension of scalars along
1230	      \(A \to R' \otimes_R A\)) and \((g')_*\) is restriction back along it; the unit
1231	      on the global \(A\)-module \(M\) is the base-change unit
1232	      \(m \mapsto 1 \otimes m = (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\).
1233	    \item[(2)] \emph{Apply \(f_* = \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\) and reindex.}
1234	      Restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\) leaves the assignment
1235	      \(m \mapsto 1 \otimes m\) unchanged on elements; the pseudofunctor identities
1236	      \((g'f)_* = (f'g)_* = g_* f'_*\) reindex the target as the \(R'\)-module
1237	      \(f'_*(g')^*\widetilde M = (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\) of
1238	      Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read}.
1239	    \item[(3)] \emph{Transpose under \((g^* \dashv g_*)\) for \(\psi\).} The transpose
1240	      of an \(R\)-linear map \(u : \operatorname{restr}_\varphi M \to
1241	      \operatorname{restr}_\psi N\) is the unique \(R'\)-linear
1242	      \(\widehat u : R' \otimes_R M \to N\) with
1243	      \(\widehat u(r' \otimes m) = r' \cdot u(m)\); the source reads as
1244	      \(R' \otimes_R M\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_domain_read}). Hence
1245	      \(r' \otimes m \mapsto r' \cdot \bigl((1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\bigr)
1246	      = (r' \otimes 1) \otimes m\), using that the \(R'\)-action scales the
1247	      \(R'\)-factor.
1248	  \end{itemize}
1249	  This is exactly the ``boils down to the equality'' computation of the source.
1250	\end{proof}

1386	[Affine base change]
1387	  \label{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}

1393	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
1394	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I)
1395	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L906--918).
1396	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
1397	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
1398	  % Assume $f$ is affine.
1399	  % In this case $f_*\mathcal{F} \cong Rf_*\mathcal{F}$ is
1400	  % a quasi-coherent sheaf, and for every base change diagram
1401	  % (\ref{equation-base-change-diagram})
1402	  % we have
1403	  % $$
1404	  % g^*f_*\mathcal{F} = f'_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}.
1405	  % $$"
1406	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
1407	  change''.}
1408	  Assume \(f : X \to S\) is an affine morphism and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a
1409	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Then for every base-change square as
1410	  above the canonical map of
1411	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an isomorphism
1412	  \[
1413	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \;\cong\; f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr).
1414	  \]
1415	  The quasi-coherence hypothesis on \(\mathcal{F}\) is essential and is not a mere
1416	  convenience: the proof works over an affine open by writing
1417	  \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) for a module \(M\), and a general
1418	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module need not be of this form
1419	  \(\widetilde{M}\); without it neither the affine description of the pushforward
1420	  nor the tensor-associativity computation below is available, and the conclusion
1421	  fails.
1422	\end{lemma}
1423	
1424	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The vanishing of higher direct images is
1425	% Lemma \ref{lemma-relative-affine-vanishing}.
1426	% The statement is local on $S$ and $S'$. Hence we may
1427	% assume $X = \Spec(A)$, $S = \Spec(R)$,
1428	% $S' = \Spec(R')$ and $\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}$
1429	% for some $A$-module $M$.
1430	% We use Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-widetilde-pullback}
1431	% to describe pullbacks and pushforwards of $\mathcal{F}$.
1432	% Namely, $X' = \Spec(R' \otimes_R A)$ and
1433	% $\mathcal{F}'$ is the quasi-coherent sheaf associated
1434	% to $(R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M$.
1435	% Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the
1436	% equality
1437	% $$
1438	% (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M
1439	% $$
1440	% as $R'$-modules."
1441	% NOTE: This affine lemma is closed directly on global sections, via the two named
1442	% obligations: lem:base_change_map_affine_local (locality reduction on the affine
1443	% opens of S') and lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange (the section-
1444	% level value of the base-change map is cancelBaseChange). Both proved affine tilde
1445	% dictionaries -- lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso and lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso --
1446	% feed the second obligation. cancelBaseChange is in Mathlib with no flatness; the
1447	% affine case carries no flatness hypothesis. No Čech infrastructure is used here.
1448	\begin{proof}
1449	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens, lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso, lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso, lem:base_change_map_affine_local, lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange}
1450	  The proof is the composite of the two named obligations, taken \emph{directly on
1451	  global sections}.
1452	
1453	  \medskip
1454	  \emph{First reduction (locality on \(S'\)).} By
1455	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens} it suffices to prove that the
1456	  base-change map of Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an
1457	  isomorphism on sections over every affine open of \(S'\). This is exactly the
1458	  locality reduction Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_map_affine_local}: restricting the
1459	  cartesian square to an affine open \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(R') \subseteq S'\) and
1460	  a chosen affine \(\operatorname{Spec}(R) \subseteq S\) containing \(g(U)\), the
1461	  hypothesis \([\mathrm{IsAffineHom}\ f]\) makes \(X\) restrict to
1462	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and quasi-coherence of \(\mathcal{F}\) makes it restrict
1463	  to \(\widetilde{M}\) for an \(A\)-module \(M\). (It is here that quasi-coherence is
1464	  used: it is what licenses writing \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\).) Computing the
1465	  fibre product gives \(X' = \operatorname{Spec}(R' \otimes_R A)\) and
1466	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) the tilde of the \((R' \otimes_R A)\)-module
1467	  \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\). The matter is thus reduced to the affine--affine
1468	  model, where the base-change map is a morphism \(\alpha\) of quasi-coherent
1469	  \((\operatorname{Spec} R')\)-modules.
1470	
1471	  \medskip
1472	  \emph{Passage to global sections.} The functor
1473	  \(\widetilde{(-)} : \operatorname{ModuleCat}(R') \to
1474	  (\operatorname{Spec} R').\mathrm{Modules}\) is fully faithful with essential image
1475	  the quasi-coherent modules; equivalently the counit
1476	  \(\widetilde{\Gamma(\mathcal{N})} \to \mathcal{N}\) is an isomorphism exactly when
1477	  \(\mathcal{N}\) is quasi-coherent. Source and target of \(\alpha\) are
1478	  quasi-coherent, so both counits are isomorphisms and, by their naturality,
1479	  \[
1480	    \mathrm{IsIso}\,\alpha
1481	    \;\Longleftrightarrow\;
1482	    \mathrm{IsIso}\bigl(\Gamma(\alpha)\bigr),
1483	  \]
1484	  where \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) is a concrete \(R'\)-linear map in
1485	  \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}(R')\). The whole computation therefore stays at the level
1486	  of modules.
1487	
1488	  \medskip
1489	  \emph{Section-level identification (the second obligation).} By the section-level
1490	  computation Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange}, the map
1491	  \(\Gamma(\alpha)\), with its domain \(\Gamma(g^*(f_*\widetilde{M}))\) identified with
1492	  \(R' \otimes_R M\) and its codomain \(\Gamma(f'_*(g')^*\widetilde{M})\) identified
1493	  with \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\) through the two dictionaries
1494	  Lemmas~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso} and~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso},
1495	  \emph{is} the canonical cancellation isomorphism
1496	  \[
1497	    \operatorname{cancelBaseChange} :
1498	    (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M,
1499	    \qquad (r' \otimes a) \otimes m \;\mapsto\; r' \otimes (a \cdot m)
1500	  \]
1501	  of \(R'\)-modules (Mathlib's
1502	  \texttt{TensorProduct.AlgebraTensorModule.cancelBaseChange}). This is the entire
1503	  content of the affine close, and it is performed \emph{directly on the module of
1504	  global sections} — there is no separate abstract adjoint-mate identification at the
1505	  level of sheaves. Because \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\) is an isomorphism with
1506	  no flatness hypothesis whatsoever, \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) is an isomorphism, hence so is
1507	  \(\alpha\) on the affine chart, and by the locality reduction so is the global
1508	  base-change map. Flatness of \(g\) plays no role in the affine lemma; it enters only
1509	  in the global statement Theorem~\ref{thm:flat_base_change_pushforward}, where it is
1510	  used to commute \(- \otimes_A B\) with the finite equalizer computing \(H^0\).
1511	\end{proof}
1512	
1513	\section{Flat base change for the pushforward}
1514	
1515	The global statement is assembled from the affine lemma and the single homological
1516	input that flatness preserves the finite equalizer computing \(H^0\). That input is
1517	supplied verbatim by Mathlib, recorded here as a dependency anchor.
1518	
1519	\begin{lemma}[Flat base change preserves finite equalizers]
1520	  \label{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}
1521	  \lean{LinearMap.tensorEqLocusEquiv}
1522	  \mathlibok
1523	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1524	  Let \(A \to B\) be a flat ring homomorphism (more generally, let \(B\) be a flat
1525	  \(A\)-module that is also an \(A\)-algebra, with the scalar-tower compatibility),
1526	  and let \(f, g : N \rightrightarrows P\) be a pair of \(A\)-linear maps. Write
1527	  \(\operatorname{eq}(f, g) = \{x \in N : f(x) = g(x)\}\) for their equalizer. Then the
1528	  canonical \(B\)-linear comparison map
1529	  \[
1530	    B \otimes_A \operatorname{eq}(f, g)
1531	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1532	    \operatorname{eq}\bigl(\mathbf{1}_B \otimes f,\ \mathbf{1}_B \otimes g\bigr)
1533	  \]
1534	  is an isomorphism; that is, base change along a flat algebra commutes with the
1535	  formation of the finite equalizer (equivalently, of the kernel: the case
1536	  \(g = 0\) is \texttt{LinearMap.tensorKerEquiv}, underlain by
1537	  \texttt{Module.Flat.ker\_lTensor\_eq} and \texttt{Module.Flat.eqLocus\_lTensor\_eq}).
1538	  Since \(- \otimes_A B\) also preserves finite products, it follows that
1539	  \(- \otimes_A B\) carries the degree-\(0\)/\(1\) equalizer
1540	  \(\prod_i \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F}) \rightrightarrows
1541	    \prod_{i,j} \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F})\) of a finite cover to the corresponding
1542	  equalizer of the base-changed cover.
1543	\end{lemma}
1544	
1545	\begin{theorem}\leanok
1546	[Flat base change, $i=0$ case]
1547	  \label{thm:flat_base_change_pushforward}
1548	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.flatBaseChange_pushforward_isIso}
1549	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:affine_base_change_pushforward,
1550	        lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}
1551	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 02KH (Lemma "Flat base change", part for
1552	  % $i = 0$) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L947--970, and
1553	  % references/stacks-coherent.md).
1554	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Consider a cartesian diagram of schemes
1555	  % $$
1556	  % \xymatrix{
1557	  % X' \ar[d]_{f'} \ar[r]_{g'} & X \ar[d]^f \\
1558	  % S' \ar[r]^g & S
1559	  % }
1560	  % $$
1561	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
1562	  % with pullback $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$.
1563	  % Assume that $g$ is flat and that $f$ is quasi-compact and quasi-separated.
1564	  % For any $i \geq 0$
1565	  % \begin{enumerate}
1566	  % \item the base change map of
1567	  % Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-base-change-map-flat-case}
1568	  % is an isomorphism
1569	  % $$
1570	  % g^*R^if_*\mathcal{F} \longrightarrow R^if'_*\mathcal{F}',
1571	  % $$
1572	  % \item if $S = \Spec(A)$ and $S' = \Spec(B)$, then
1573	  % $H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B = H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')$.
1574	  % \end{enumerate}"
1575	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Tag 02KH (``Flat base change''), the
1576	  \(i = 0\) case.}
1577	  Consider the cartesian square
1578	  \[
1579	    \begin{array}{ccc}
1580	      X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
1581	      {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
1582	      S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
1583	    \end{array}
1584	  \]
1585	  with \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and
1586	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). Assume that \(g\) is flat and that \(f\)
1587	  is quasi-compact and quasi-separated. Then the base-change map of
1588	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an isomorphism
1589	  \[
1590	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
1591	    f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr) = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
1592	  \]
1593	  Equivalently, in the affine situation \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\),
1594	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat, the comparison map
1595	  \[
1596	    H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
1597	    H^0(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)
1598	  \]
1599	  is an isomorphism, where \(X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)}
1600	  \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) and \(\mathcal{F}_B\) is the pullback of
1601	  \(\mathcal{F}\).
1602	
1603	  The hypothesis that \(\mathcal{F}\) is quasi-coherent is essential: it is what
1604	  makes \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) quasi-coherent on the affine base and thus the tilde of
1605	  a module, and it is what lets the affine reduction
1606	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}) write \(\mathcal{F}\) as
1607	  \(\widetilde{M}\) over each affine open. Without it the comparison map need not be
1608	  an isomorphism.
1609	\end{theorem}
1610	
1611	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Having said this, part (1) follows from part (2). Namely,
1612	% part (1) is local on $S'$ and hence we may assume $S$
1613	% and $S'$ are affine. In other words, we have $S = \Spec(A)$
1614	% and $S' = \Spec(B)$ as in (2).
1615	% Then since $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is quasi-coherent
1616	% (Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images}),
1617	% it is the quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_S$-module associated to the
1618	% $A$-module $H^0(S, R^if_*\mathcal{F}) = H^i(X, \mathcal{F})$
1619	% (equality by
1620	% Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}).
1621	% Similarly, $R^if'_*\mathcal{F}'$ is the quasi-coherent
1622	% $\mathcal{O}_{S'}$-module associated to the $B$-module
1623	% $H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')$. Since pullback by $g$ corresponds
1624	% to $- \otimes_A B$ on modules
1625	% (Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-widetilde-pullback})
1626	% we see that it suffices to prove (2).
1627	%
1628	% Let $A \to B$ be a flat ring homomorphism.
1629	% Let $X$ be a quasi-compact and quasi-separated scheme over $A$.
1630	% Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
1631	% Set $X_B = X \times_{\Spec(A)} \Spec(B)$ and denote
1632	% $\mathcal{F}_B$ the pullback of $\mathcal{F}$.
1633	% We are trying to show that the map
1634	% $$
1635	% H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \longrightarrow H^i(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)
1636	% $$
1637	% (given by the reference in the statement of the lemma)
1638	% is an isomorphism.
1639	%
1640	% In case $X$ is separated, choose an affine open covering
1641	% $\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \ldots \cup U_t$ and recall that
1642	% $$
1643	% \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F}),
1644	% $$
1645	% see
1646	% Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent}.
1647	% If $\mathcal{U}_B : X_B = (U_1)_B \cup \ldots \cup (U_t)_B$ we obtain
1648	% by base change, then it is still the case that each $(U_i)_B$ is affine
1649	% and that $X_B$ is separated. Thus we obtain
1650	% $$
1651	% \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) = H^p(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B).
1652	% $$
1653	% We have the following relation between the {\v C}ech complexes
1654	% $$
1655	% \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) =
1656	% \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B
1657	% $$
1658	% as follows from
1659	% Lemma \ref{lemma-affine-base-change}.
1660	% Since $A \to B$ is flat, the same thing remains true on taking cohomology.
1661	%
1662	% In case $X$ is quasi-separated, choose an affine open covering
1663	% $\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \ldots \cup U_t$. We will use the
1664	% {\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral sequence
1665	% Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-cech-spectral-sequence}.
1666	% The reader who wishes to avoid this spectral sequence
1667	% can use Mayer-Vietoris and induction on $t$ as in the proof of
1668	% Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images}."
1669	% NOTE: For the $i = 0$ case the argument is Čech-free: $H^0(X,\mathcal{F})$ is the
1670	% sheaf-condition equalizer of a finite affine cover (Čech degree 0/1, NOT Čech
1671	% cohomology -- no Leray, no spectral sequence), each term base-changes by the affine
1672	% lemma lem:affine_base_change_pushforward, and flatness of $A \to B$ commutes
1673	% $-\otimes_A B$ with that finite equalizer via lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib
1674	% (Mathlib's LinearMap.tensorEqLocusEquiv). The quasi-separated case is the
1675	% Mayer--Vietoris induction on the number of cover members named in the source quote.
1676	\begin{proof}
1677	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:affine_base_change_pushforward, lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}
1678	  For \(i = 0\) the argument is {\v C}ech-\emph{free}: it uses only the sheaf
1679	  condition (the degree-\(0\)/\(1\) part of {\v C}ech, i.e.\ an equalizer of a finite
1680	  cover), the affine lemma, and the fact that flat base change commutes with that
1681	  finite equalizer. There is no Leray sequence and no {\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral
1682	  sequence.
1683	
1684	  \medskip
1685	  \emph{Reduction to the affine target.} The statement is local on \(S'\), so we
1686	  may assume \(S\) and \(S'\) are affine, say \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and
1687	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat. Because \(f\) is
1688	  quasi-compact and quasi-separated, \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) is quasi-coherent on
1689	  \(S\); it is therefore the sheaf associated to the \(A\)-module
1690	  \(H^0(S, f_*\mathcal{F}) = H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\). Likewise
1691	  \(f'_*\mathcal{F}'\) is the sheaf on \(S'\) associated to the \(B\)-module
1692	  \(H^0(X', \mathcal{F}') = \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\). Since pullback along \(g\)
1693	  corresponds to \(- \otimes_A B\) on modules, the base-change map becomes the
1694	  comparison map of \(B\)-modules
1695	  \[
1696	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \longrightarrow \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B),
1697	    \qquad X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)} \operatorname{Spec}(B),
1698	  \]
1699	  and it suffices to prove that this map is an isomorphism.
1700	
1701	  \medskip
1702	  \emph{\(H^0\) as a finite equalizer (the sheaf condition).} Choose a finite affine
1703	  open cover \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\) (possible since \(X\) is
1704	  quasi-compact). The sheaf condition for \(\mathcal{F}\) on this cover presents the
1705	  global sections as the equalizer of the two restriction maps
1706	  \[
1707	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})
1708	    \;=\;
1709	    \operatorname{eq}\Bigl(
1710	      \textstyle\prod_i \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F})
1711	      \;\rightrightarrows\;
1712	      \textstyle\prod_{i,j} \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F})
1713	    \Bigr),
1714	    \qquad U_{ij} = U_i \cap U_j,
1715	  \]
1716	  where the two parallel maps are \(s \mapsto (s_i|_{U_{ij}})\) and
1717	  \(s \mapsto (s_j|_{U_{ij}})\). This is precisely Čech degree \(0\) into degree
1718	  \(1\); it is the sheaf axiom and requires \emph{no} separatedness of \(X\) and no
1719	  higher cohomology.
1720	
1721	  \medskip
1722	  \emph{The separated case.} Suppose first that \(X\) is separated over \(A\). Then
1723	  every intersection \(U_{ij}\) is affine. Base changing the cover yields a finite
1724	  affine cover \(\mathcal{U}_B : X_B = (U_1)_B \cup \dots \cup (U_t)_B\) of \(X_B\),
1725	  and the same sheaf-condition equalizer presents \(\Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\). The
1726	  affine lemma Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward} (in its
1727	  \(i = 0\)/global-sections form) identifies each base-changed term with the original
1728	  term tensored over \(A\) with \(B\):
1729	  \[
1730	    \Gamma\bigl((U_i)_B, \mathcal{F}_B\bigr) \cong \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B,
1731	    \qquad
1732	    \Gamma\bigl((U_{ij})_B, \mathcal{F}_B\bigr) \cong \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B,
1733	  \]
1734	  compatibly with the two restriction maps. Hence the equalizer diagram for \(X_B\) is
1735	  the diagram for \(X\) with \(- \otimes_A B\) applied (using that \(- \otimes_A B\)
1736	  commutes with the finite products \(\prod_i\), \(\prod_{i,j}\)). Because \(A \to B\)
1737	  is flat, base change commutes with the formation of this finite equalizer
1738	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}, i.e.\ Mathlib's
1739	  \texttt{LinearMap.tensorEqLocusEquiv}):
1740	  \[
1741	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B
1742	    = \operatorname{eq}(\cdots) \otimes_A B
1743	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1744	    \operatorname{eq}(\cdots \otimes_A B)
1745	    = \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B).
1746	  \]
1747	  This is exactly the comparison map of the reduction step, so it is an isomorphism.
1748	
1749	  \medskip
1750	  \emph{The quasi-separated case.} In general \(X\) need only be quasi-separated. We
1751	  argue by Mayer--Vietoris induction on the number \(t\) of members of a finite affine
1752	  cover \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\), each step reducing to the
1753	  separated/affine case. For \(t = 1\), \(X = U_1\) is affine and the claim is the
1754	  affine lemma. For \(t > 1\), set \(V = U_t\) (affine) and
1755	  \(W = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_{t-1}\) (an open covered by \(t - 1\) affines). Their
1756	  intersection \(W \cap V = \bigcup_{i < t}(U_i \cap U_t)\) is covered by the
1757	  \(t - 1\) opens \(U_i \cap U_t\), each of which is quasi-compact and separated
1758	  (an open of the affine, hence separated, \(U_i\)); thus \(W \cap V\) is separated
1759	  and quasi-compact, and flat base change holds for it by the separated case. The
1760	  two-member cover \(\{W, V\}\) of \(X\) gives the left-exact Mayer--Vietoris
1761	  sequence — itself the sheaf-condition equalizer for that cover —
1762	  \[
1763	    0 \longrightarrow \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})
1764	      \longrightarrow \Gamma(W, \mathcal{F}) \oplus \Gamma(V, \mathcal{F})
1765	      \longrightarrow \Gamma(W \cap V, \mathcal{F}),
1766	  \]
1767	  and likewise for \(X_B\) with \(\{W_B, V_B\}\). Flatness of \(A \to B\) makes
1768	  \(- \otimes_A B\) preserve this finite kernel/equalizer
1769	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}); since flat base change holds for
1770	  \(V\) (affine), for \(W\) (inductive hypothesis, \(t - 1\) members), and for
1771	  \(W \cap V\) (separated), it follows for \(X\). In degree \(0\) this gives
1772	  \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \cong \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\), as required.
1773	\end{proof}

7	% relative-spectrum functor \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) : \mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)^{op}
8	% \to \mathrm{Sch}/X used by the relative Picard functor on a product C \times_k T.

14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	\label{sec:relspec_setup}
16	
17	The Route~A construction of \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety is
18	organised around the relative Picard functor
19	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\)
20	on the category of \(k\)-schemes. Representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) requires the line-bundle
21	pullback construction on a relative curve \(C \times_k T \to T\), which in turn rests on a
22	\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras
23	\(\mathcal{A}\), the \emph{relative spectrum}
24	\(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\). This chapter packages the construction
25	of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(-)\) and its core properties --- universal property and affine
26	base --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
27	functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
28	identity-component / dimension theory of \(J\) (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
29	(\S~``Out of scope'' below).
30	
31	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section ``Relative spectrum via glueing'' and ``Relative
32	% spectrum as a functor'' (tags 01LL, 01LO, 01LQ, 01LR, 01LS, available locally at
33	% references/stacks-constructions.tex L309-L720). The Hartshorne reference is
34	% [Hartshorne], II Exercise 5.17 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).
35	
36	\section{Quasi-coherent sheaves of algebras}
37	\label{sec:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
38	
39	\begin{definition}\leanok
40	[Quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra]
41	  \label{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
42	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QcohAlgebra}
43	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section 01LL ``Relative spectrum via glueing'',
44	  % Situation ``situation-relative-spec''
45	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L312-L318).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "Here \(S\) is a scheme, and \(\mathcal{A}\) is a quasi-coherent
48	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra. This means that \(\mathcal{A}\) is a
49	  %  sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebras which is quasi-coherent as an
50	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module."
51	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LL (situation-relative-spec).}
52	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. A \emph{quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras} is a
53	  sheaf \(\mathcal{A}\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras (i.e. an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
54	  equipped with \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear multiplication and unit morphisms satisfying
55	  associativity, commutativity, and the unit law as sheaf morphisms) whose underlying
56	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is quasi-coherent. We write \(\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)\) for the
57	  category of quasi-coherent sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras and
58	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra morphisms.
59	\end{definition}
60

63	% to a 3-field structure

65	%     `  sheaf        : TopCat.Sheaf CommRingCat.{u} X.toPresheafedSpace`
66	%     `  unit         : X.sheaf ⟶ sheaf`
67	%     `  coequifibered : NatTrans.Coequifibered`
68	%     `    (Functor.whiskerLeft (AffineZariskiSite.toOpensFunctor X).op unit.hom)`

73	% relative-spectrum scheme) via the dense-subsite equivalence
74	% `AffineZariskiSite.sheafEquiv`. This is the predicate Mathlib's
75	% `AffineZariskiSite.relativeGluingData` consumes, so the iter-179
76	% `RelativeSpec`/`structureMorphism` bodies (now real Mathlib values) close
77	% against it.
78	
79	\section{The relative-spectrum scheme}
80	\label{sec:relspec_scheme}
81	
82	The construction is given affine-locally: on every affine open \(U = \Spec R \subseteq X\) one
83	sets \(\pi^{-1}(U) := \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) regarded as an \(R\)-algebra, and the local pieces
84	glue compatibly because \(\mathcal{A}\) is quasi-coherent (the transition isomorphism
85	\(\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)\) for an inclusion
86	\(V = \Spec S \subseteq U\) gives an open immersion of the corresponding Specs).
87	
88	\begin{theorem}\leanok
89	[Relative spectrum exists]
90	  \label{thm:relative_spec_exists}
91	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec}
92	  \uses{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
93	
94	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec)
95	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L381-L405).
96	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
97	  % "In Situation REF.
98	  %  There exists a morphism of schemes
99	  %  \(\)
100	  %  \pi : \underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow S
101	  %  \(\)
102	  %  with the following properties:
103	  %  \begin{enumerate}

105	  %  \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \to \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\), and
106	  %  \item for \(U \subset U' \subset S\) affine open the composition
107	  %  \(\)
108	  %  \xymatrix{

113	  %  }
114	  %  \(\)
115	  %  is the open immersion of Lemma REF above.
116	  %  \end{enumerate}
117	  %  Moreover, \(\underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A})\)
118	  %  is unique up to unique isomorphism over \(S\)."
119	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec);
120	  cf.\ [Hartshorne], II~Ex.~5.17(a).}
121	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra
122	  (\cref{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}). There exists a scheme
123	  \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) together with an affine morphism
124	  \(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\) such that
125	  \begin{enumerate}
126	    \item for every affine open \(U \subseteq X\), there is an isomorphism
127	      \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \xrightarrow{\sim} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\);
128	    \item for \(U \subseteq U' \subseteq X\) both affine open, the composite
129	      $\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \xrightarrow{i_U^{-1}} \pi^{-1}(U) \hookrightarrow
130	      \pi^{-1}(U') \xrightarrow{i_{U'}} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))$
131	      coincides with the open immersion induced by the restriction
132	      \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\).
133	  \end{enumerate}
134	  The pair \((\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}), \pi)\) is unique up to unique isomorphism
135	  over \(X\).

138	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Follows immediately from
139	% Lemmas REF,
140	% REF, and
141	% REF.
142	% Uniqueness is stated in the last sentence of
143	% Lemma REF."
144	\begin{proof}
145	  
146	  
147	\leanok
148	  By the open-cover gluing principle for schemes (Mathlib
149	  \texttt{Scheme.GlueData} / \texttt{AffineScheme.glueOpens}) it suffices to specify
150	  affine local pieces \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) for each affine open \(U \subseteq X\) and
151	  open-immersion transition data
152	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \hookrightarrow \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\) for \(U \subseteq U'\)
153	  satisfying the cocycle condition. The transition morphisms come from the ring maps
154	  \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\); quasi-coherence yields the cartesian square
155	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) = U \times_{U'} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\), so each transition is an
156	  open immersion. Transitivity (Stacks lemma-transitive-spec) gives the cocycle condition.
157	  Uniqueness of the glued scheme is the standard property of the gluing functor.
158	\end{proof}

231	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra.

255	  \leanok
256	  % NOTE (iter-179 review): supersedes the iter-176 placeholder-discharge note.
257	  % The iter-179 Block A refactor replaces the iter-176 placeholder bodies with
258	  % the Mathlib values ``(AffineZariskiSite.relativeGluingData _).glued`` /
259	  % ``.toBase``; iter-179 Lane B closes the Lean ``UniversalProperty`` proof
260	  % via ``isAffineHom_of_forall_exists_isAffineOpen`` +

262	  % ``isAffineOpen_opensRange`` — a real proof against the gluing infrastructure,
263	  % NOT against a trivial identity placeholder. The Lean type remains the
264	  % strictly weaker ``IsAffineHom (structureMorphism 𝒜)'' rather than the
265	  % Yoneda-bijection form pinned above (iter-174+ commitment; still pending
266	  % iter-180+). Consumers may now rely on the affineness conclusion, but the
267	  % Yoneda-bijection witness is still deferred.
268	  Following Stacks tag 01LQ (proof of lemma-spec): the functor \(F\) is a Zariski sheaf
269	  because morphisms of schemes and \(\mathcal{O}\)-algebra maps both glue under open covers.
270	  Choose an affine open cover \(X = \bigcup U_i\). The subfunctor
271	  \(F_i \subseteq F\) of pairs \((f, \varphi)\) with \(f(T) \subseteq U_i\) is representable by
272	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U_i))\) via the affine-base case
273	  (\cref{thm:relative_spec_affine_base}) together with the
274	  restriction identification \(F_i \cong F_{U_i,\,\mathcal{A}|_{U_i}}\).
275	  Each \(F_i \hookrightarrow F\) is an open
276	  subfunctor (pull back \(U_i\) along \(f\)). The \(F_i\) jointly cover \(F\) since the \(U_i\)
277	  cover \(X\). The gluing of representable open subfunctors produces a representing scheme,
278	  which is canonically isomorphic to the glued scheme \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\)

287	  \label{thm:relative_spec_affine_base}

289	  \uses{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
290	  % NOTE (iter-173 review): the iter-173 file-skeleton encodes this as the
291	  % weaker ``IsAffine ((Spec R).RelativeSpec 𝒜)'' rather than the canonical
292	  % iso ``Spec_X(𝒜) ≅ Spec(Γ(X, 𝒜))'' pinned by this prose. The Lean name
293	  % ``affine_base_iff'' is also misleading (the encoded type is not an iff).
294	  % Iter-174+ refines to the iff-with-Γ statement once Γ : QcohAlgebra → CommRingCat
295	  % is available.
296	  
297	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 01LO (lemma-spec-affine)
298	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L491-L545).
299	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
300	  % "In Situation REF.
301	  %  Let \(F\) be the functor associated to \((S, \mathcal{A})\) above.
302	  %  If \(S\) is affine, then \(F\) is representable by the
303	  %  affine scheme \(\Spec(\Gamma(S, \mathcal{A}))\)."
304	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LO (lemma-spec-affine).}
305	  Let \(X = \Spec R\) be an affine scheme and let \(\mathcal{A}\) be a quasi-coherent
306	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra. Set \(A := \Gamma(X, \mathcal{A})\), regarded as an
307	  \(R\)-algebra. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of \(X\)-schemes
308	  \[
309	    \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \;\;\cong\;\; \Spec(A).
310	  \]
311	  In particular, on an affine base the relative spectrum reduces to the absolute
312	  spectrum of the global sections.

315	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Write \(S = \Spec(R)\) and \(A = \Gamma(S, \mathcal{A})\).
316	% Then \(A\) is an \(R\)-algebra and \(\mathcal{A} = \widetilde A\).
317	% The ring map \(R \to A\) gives rise to a canonical map
318	% \(\)
319	% f_{univ} : \Spec(A)
320	% \longrightarrow
321	% S = \Spec(R).
322	% \(\)
323	% We have
324	% \(f_{univ}^*\mathcal{A} =  \widetilde{A \otimes_R A}\)
325	% by Schemes, Lemma REF.
326	% Hence there is a canonical map
327	% \(\)
328	% \varphi_{univ} :
329	% f_{univ}^*\mathcal{A} = \widetilde{A \otimes_R A}
330	% \longrightarrow
331	% \widetilde A = \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(A)}
332	% \(\)
333	% coming from the \(A\)-module map \(A \otimes_R A \to A\),
334	% \(a \otimes a' \mapsto aa'\). We claim that the pair
335	% \((f_{univ}, \varphi_{univ})\) represents \(F\) in this case."
336	\begin{proof}
337	  
338	  \leanok
339	  % NOTE (iter-179 review): supersedes the iter-176 placeholder-discharge note.
340	  % The iter-176 ``change IsAffine (Spec R); inferInstance`` form is now stale
341	  % — iter-179 Lane B closes via ``UniversalProperty 𝒜`` + ``isAffine_of_isAffineHom``
342	  % against the iter-179 Mathlib-backed ``RelativeSpec``/``structureMorphism``
343	  % bodies. The Lean type remains the strictly weaker ``IsAffine`` rather than
344	  % the canonical iso ``Spec_X(𝒜) ≅ Spec(Γ(X,𝒜))'' named below (iter-174+
345	  % commitment; still pending iter-180+).
346	  Write \(X = \Spec R\) and \(A = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{A})\). Quasi-coherence of
347	  \(\mathcal{A}\) gives \(\mathcal{A} = \widetilde{A}\) as \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
348	  (with the \(R\)-algebra structure on \(A\) inducing the algebra structure on
349	  \(\widetilde A\)). The ring map \(R \to A\) gives a morphism
350	  \(f_{\mathrm{univ}} : \Spec A \to \Spec R = X\) and a canonical
351	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A}\)-algebra map
352	  $\varphi_{\mathrm{univ}} : f_{\mathrm{univ}}^* \mathcal{A} = \widetilde{A \otimes_R A}
353	  \to \mathcal{O}_{\Spec A} = \widetilde A$ given by multiplication
354	  \(a \otimes a' \mapsto aa'\). We verify \((\Spec A, f_{\mathrm{univ}}, \varphi_{\mathrm{univ}})\)
355	  represents \(F\) on the affine base: given a test scheme \(T\) and a pair
356	  \((f, \varphi)\), the composite
357	  $A = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{A}) \xrightarrow{f^\sharp}
358	  \Gamma(T, f^*\mathcal{A}) \xrightarrow{\varphi} \Gamma(T, \mathcal{O}_T)$
359	  is a ring map \(A \to \Gamma(T, \mathcal{O}_T)\) and hence corresponds to a morphism

362	  \texttt{Scheme.Hom.toSpec}). One checks this assignment inverts the universal map

364	  \(\varphi = a^* \varphi_{\mathrm{univ}}\).

367	\section{Lean encoding}
368	\label{sec:relspec_lean_encoding}
369	
370	The Lean target file is the new \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean}.
371	The construction proceeds by gluing absolute affine pieces: for each affine open
372	\(U \subseteq X\) with \(U = \Spec R\) and \(A := \mathcal{A}(U)\) regarded as an
373	\(R\)-algebra, the local piece is \(\Spec A\) with structure morphism \(\Spec A \to \Spec R = U\)
374	induced by the algebra unit \(R \to A\). Mathlib provides \texttt{IsAffineOpen.toScheme} for
375	the affine-open inclusion and the absolute \texttt{Scheme.Spec} functor for the local
376	pieces; the transition isomorphisms come from quasi-coherence of \(\mathcal{A}\) via
377	\texttt{QuasiCoherent.tensorIso} (or the explicit
378	\(\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)\) for \(V = \Spec S \subseteq U\)).
379	The gluing backbone is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.GlueData} (general construction) or
380	the convenience wrapper \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme.glueOpens}; the cocycle
381	condition reduces to Stacks \texttt{lemma-transitive-spec}. The universal property
382	(\cref{thm:relative_spec_univ}) is expressed as a
383	\texttt{CategoryTheory.Functor.RepresentableBy} witness, providing the natural bijection
384	$\Hom_X(T, \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})) \cong
385	\Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X\text{-alg}}(\mathcal{A}, g_*\mathcal{O}_T)$ as a
386	\texttt{Yoneda}-style structure. No new core axiom is required; the entire chapter is a
387	definitional/structural construction on top of existing Mathlib affine-scheme
388	infrastructure.
389

24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:quot_setup}
26	
27	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
28	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
29	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
30	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
31	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
32	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
33	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
34	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
35	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness)
36	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
37	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
38	This chapter develops the foundations of that picture: the Hilbert
39	polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}), the Quot functor
40	(\cref{def:quot_functor}), and the Grassmannian scheme
41	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) with its representability
42	(\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
43	
44	% SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1;
45	% FGA Explained Ch.~5, AMS MSM 123 (read from
46	% references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L287-L500).
47	% The Hartshorne reference for the polynomial-eventually
48	% property of Euler characteristics is [Hartshorne], III.5.2 (the scanned
49	% PDF at references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf has no text layer, so
50	% the verbatim source-quote is taken from
51	% Nitsure \S 1; Hartshorne III.5.2 is cited in prose as the textbook
52	% reference).
53	
54	\section{Hilbert polynomial of a coherent sheaf}
55	\label{sec:hilbert_polynomial}
56

59	  \label{def:hilbert_polynomial}

61	  % ENCODING PIVOT (iter-003 strategy decision — see STRATEGY.md ## Routes QUOT):

64	  % `Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly` (Hartshorne I.7.5), NOT via the cohomological
65	  % Euler characteristic χ written below. The two agree for m≫0 (Serre vanishing), but
66	  % the χ route requires all Hⁱ (i>0) and breaks the Čech-independent leg; the graded
67	  % route is Čech-independent. The χ prose/quotes below are RETAINED FOR REFERENCE and
68	  % must be rewritten to the graded encoding (queued blueprint-writer task) before any
69	  % QUOT prover dispatch (QUOT is gated behind FBC/GF regardless). Do NOT formalize the
70	  % χ form.

74	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
75	  % "Let \(X\) be a finite type scheme over
76	  %  a field \(k\), together with a line bundle \(L\).
77	  %  Recall that if \(F\) is a coherent sheaf on
78	  %  \(X\) whose support is proper over \(k\), then
79	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\) of \(F\) is defined by
80	  %  the function

83	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
